title: Quiz: Pre-trained Quantum Models
description: Test your understanding of creating and using pre-trained quantum models

questions.question: What should a pre-trainable quantum architecture have?

options.text: Random structure that changes each run
Modular structure with separable encoder and task layers

questions.explanation: Pre-trainable architectures should have modular structure where encoder layers learn transferable representations and task layers can be adapted for new tasks.

questions.question: What should model checkpoints include besides parameters?
questions.explanation: Checkpoints should include architecture information and metadata (training config, performance metrics) to help select appropriate pre-trained models.

questions.question: What is a quantum model zoo?

options.text: A zoo that uses quantum computers
A repository of pre-trained quantum models

questions.explanation: A quantum model zoo is a repository where pre-trained quantum models are stored, organized, and shared for reuse in transfer learning.

questions.question: High expressibility of a pre-trained model guarantees good transfer performance.
questions.explanation: False. High expressibility means diverse outputs but doesn't guarantee the learned representations are useful for the target task. Task-specific evaluation is needed.

questions.question: What is the 'adapter layers' strategy?

options.text: Remove all layers and start fresh
Add small trainable layers between frozen encoder and task layers

questions.explanation: Adapter layers are small trainable modules added between frozen pre-trained layers to adapt the model with minimal additional parameters.

questions.question: What is knowledge distillation in the context of transfer?

options.text: Transferring knowledge from a larger teacher to a smaller student

questions.explanation: Knowledge distillation trains a smaller student model to mimic the outputs of a larger pre-trained teacher model, transferring knowledge efficiently.

questions.question: Why might pre-trained simulator models need adaptation for hardware?

options.text: Hardware has noise that simulators don't

questions.explanation: Real quantum hardware has noise (decoherence, gate errors) that noise-free simulators don't model, requiring adaptation when transferring.

questions.question: What are common pitfalls in using pre-trained quantum models? (Select all)

options.text: Mismatched architectures between source and target
Using too high learning rate for transferred layers
Always using the same model for all tasks

questions.explanation: Common pitfalls include architecture mismatch, not validating against scratch training, and using too high LR. Using appropriate models for different tasks is actually good practice.
